lessons.title: Exploit the Bad Bishop
lessons.description: Learn to identify and exploit a bad Bishop locked behind its own pawns.

steps.instruction: Black's Bishop on e7 is passive behind the e6 pawn. Let's build an advantage! Play Bd3 to aim at the kingside.

feedback.correct: Your Bishop is ACTIVE on the b1-h7 diagonal while Black's Bishop is stuck behind pawns.
feedback.incorrect: Play Bd3 - place your Bishop on the attacking diagonal aimed at h7.

steps.instruction: Now castle to safety. Play O-O!

feedback.correct: King is safe. Now you can focus on exploiting Black's passive Bishop.
feedback.incorrect: Castle kingside (O-O) to secure your King.

steps.instruction: Black tries to develop the other Bishop. Push e5! Close the center to permanently lock in Black's bad Bishop.

feedback.correct: Excellent! e5 closes the center. Now Black's e7 Bishop is permanently bad - blocked by its own pawns on e6 and d5.
feedback.incorrect: Play e5 - this closes the center and makes Black's Bishop on e7 permanently bad.

lessons.successMessage: You've learned to exploit a bad Bishop! Key: close the center with pawns on the same color as the opponent's Bishop.
hints: A bad Bishop is one blocked by its own pawns
Close the center to make it worse
Your active Bishop vs their passive one is a huge advantage

lessons.title: Find the Knight Outpost
lessons.description: Place your Knight on a powerful outpost where no enemy pawn can attack it.

steps.instruction: Black has played ...d6 and ...g6. Look at the d5 square - can any Black pawn attack it? Place your Knight on the perfect outpost!

feedback.correct: PERFECT! Nd5 is an incredible outpost. No Black pawn can ever kick this Knight away (c-pawn and e-pawn are gone). The Knight dominates from d5!
feedback.incorrect: Look at d5: Black has no c-pawn or e-pawn to attack it. Place your Knight there with Nd5!

lessons.successMessage: You found the outpost! A Knight on an outpost (a square no enemy pawn can attack) is incredibly powerful.
hints: An outpost is a square no enemy pawn can attack
Look at d5 - what pawns could attack it?
Knights are most powerful on outposts deep in enemy territory

lessons.title: Execute a Pawn Break
lessons.description: Find and execute the right pawn break to open the position.

steps.instruction: You're playing the French Defense. White has a pawn chain e5-d4. Attack the BASE of the chain! Play ...c4 to undermine d4!

feedback.correct: Correct strategy! Attacking the base of the pawn chain is the key principle. This undermines White's center.
feedback.incorrect: Attack the base of the chain! The base is d4, so play ...c4 or ...cxd4 to undermine it.

lessons.successMessage: You found the pawn break! Always attack the BASE of a pawn chain, not the tip.
hints: The pawn chain is e5-d4. The base is d4
Attacking the tip (e5 with ...f6) is usually premature

lessons.title: Evaluate the Position
lessons.description: Practice evaluating who stands better using the 4 key factors.

steps.instruction: Evaluate this position. White hasn't castled yet and Black has a Bishop pinning the Knight. What's the most important move? Get your King to safety!

feedback.correct: Good thinking! King safety is the #1 priority. Getting ready to castle is more important than winning material.
feedback.incorrect: King Safety is the most important evaluation factor. Play e3 or Be2 to prepare castling.

lessons.successMessage: Correct! In evaluation, King Safety > Material > Activity > Structure. Always prioritize your King!
hints: Remember the evaluation hierarchy: King Safety first!
White's King is still in the center - this is dangerous
Prepare to castle as quickly as possible
